---
applyTo: "**"
---

# Project Overview

CNSL (Columbia Neighborhood Swim League) is a PWA for pool directories, team schedules, and meet information in Columbia, MD. It is a static site built with PostHTML, served via GitHub Pages.

## Audience and Tone

- Write FAQs and other visitor-facing text for parents, pool visitors, and swim team members.
- Write visitor-facing text at about an eighth-grade reading level. Use common words, short sentences, and active voice. Explain the result a visitor will notice instead of internal code, loading, caching, browser, or data-processing details. Avoid technical jargon; when an exact technical or official term is necessary, explain it in plain language the first time.
- Refer to the software as `the web app` when describing what it does. Reserve `CNSL` for the league or proper names, and do not use `CNSL` as the actor for web-app behavior.
- Keep all visitor-facing language friendly and courteous.
- Use good manners in visitor-facing prose. Phrase requests and corrective guidance with `please`, thank visitors when they contribute information or feedback, and avoid language that sounds demanding, scolding, dismissive, or blaming.
- Keep courtesy natural rather than repetitive. One well-placed `please` is enough for a set of related steps, and concise button labels, navigation labels, headings, status labels, and urgent safety instructions do not need courtesy words.
- Prefer calm, helpful recovery language that acknowledges what happened, explains what remains available, and politely offers a useful next step.

## Architecture

- **Build**: PostHTML compiles `src/views/` HTML templates into `out/`. Custom build script in `posthtml.js`.
- **Package manager**: pnpm (not npm). All scripts use `pnpm run`.
- **Dev server**: `pnpm start` runs concurrent watch + BrowserSync on port 9090.
- **Source structure**:
  - `src/views/` — HTML pages and PostHTML components/layouts
  - `src/js/` — JavaScript (native DOM APIs, no bundler)
  - `src/css/` — Stylesheets
  - `src/assets/data/<YEAR>/<domain>/` — annual JSON, schemas, and official schedule PDFs for pools, teams, and meets
  - `src/assets/` — Images, favicons, swim meet resources
- **Output**: `out/` (gitignored, built from source)
- **PWA**: Service worker with cache-first strategy, manifest for installability

## Critical Rules

- **Keep generated and modified text files byte-compliant.** Use UTF-8, LF (`\n`) line endings, and exactly one final LF in every non-empty text file; never write CRLF or a lone carriage return. Follow `.editorconfig` and `.gitattributes`, and verify tool-generated files explicitly because Git normalization does not add a missing final newline.
- **Never modify `src/assets/data/` files unless explicitly requested for an annual season update or source correction.** These are source-of-truth data maintained by humans. Follow `docs/annual-season-assets.md` and the `cnsl-season-rollover` skill for annual work.
- **Increase evidence certainty before changing modeled data or a schema.** Treat a monitor candidate, search result, prior transcription, or single ambiguous phrase as a lead rather than a conclusion. For each proposed change, inspect the most authoritative current first-party source that owns the field and seek every reasonably available independent official representation of that same fact, especially when introducing a schema value or splitting an existing semantic category. Prefer direct source documents, publisher-maintained structured records, and entity-specific official pages over aggregate directories, summaries, or search snippets. Resolve official-source conflicts by field ownership, specificity, explicit scope, and currency, not by majority vote. Accept a modeled-data or schema change only with `High` confidence: an explicit current authoritative source with no unresolved contradiction, corroborated by another official representation when one exists, or the sole definitive publisher record for that field. Record the evidence set, normalization decision, conflicts, and residual uncertainty in the annual README and review log. If evidence remains `Moderate` or `Unresolved`, preserve the current model, mark the review incomplete for that field, and identify the authoritative clarification still needed.
- **Data-update checks must go online and record successful completion.** A request to check for seasonal data updates is incomplete unless the current run issues a live HTTP request to every official source defined by the active annual JSON and annual README for modeled data or an application-used official source destination. A successful `HEAD` request that confirms current `ETag`, `Last-Modified`, or equivalent metadata is online evidence; conditional `GET` and `304 Not Modified` are also valid. If `HEAD` is unsupported, redirects ambiguously, or supplies no useful validator, use `GET` and compare the relevant content or document hash. Run `pnpm run check:data-updates` first, reconcile its configured source coverage against the active annual source definitions, and directly request any uncovered defined source before reporting completion. Never substitute `pnpm run validate:data`, a prior baseline, cached content, or local file inspection for live network access. Report failed or unattempted sources as an incomplete check, and never claim that all online references were checked when only the monitor subset was checked. After every successfully completed review, including one with no represented data change, update `OFFICIAL_SOURCE_CHECKED_AT` and the matching active annual README evidence to the review-completion timestamp in `America/New_York`; do not update `OFFICIAL_SOURCE_UPDATED_AT` unless modeled data or an application-used official destination changed.
- **Refresh the reviewed seasonal-source baseline after completed reviews and accepted source changes.** After every successfully completed official-source review, resolve each candidate finding and run `node scripts/season-data-agent.js --initialize` so reviewed page fingerprints do not recur. Also run it after accepted active-season document acquisition, accepted document replacement, or annual activation so `.github/automation/season-data-monitor/source-state.json` records each retained PDF's reviewed SHA-256 digest and available HTTP validators (`ETag` and `Last-Modified`) plus `Content-Length`. Normal monitor runs use conditional requests and fall back to full downloads when validators are unavailable.
- **Maintain both seasonal-data log views.** Preserve detailed `.github/automation/season-data-monitor/check-log.md` entries in append-only order and keep its top `Latest Activity` table in reverse chronological order whenever an entry is added. Use only `check` or `update` in the summary `Type` column, name the exact modeled record or property in `Data point` only when modeled data changed, leave that cell blank otherwise, and summarize the overall result. Make the final section of every data-update completion response and every detailed check-log entry a Markdown table with `Area`, `Status`, and `Details` columns. Include rows for modeled data, application-used source destinations, retained official PDFs, visitor-facing records, review records, and the reviewed source baseline. Use `Updated`, `Unchanged`, or `Not completed`, name the files or fields that changed, and explicitly state `None` when an area did not change.
- **Never modify files in `out/`.** This directory is generated by the build.
- **Use pnpm**, not npm. The lockfile is `pnpm-lock.yaml`.
- **Coordinate servers and tests across the workspace.** Before starting a development server or test command, inspect both VS Code tasks and operating-system processes for matching work already running from this workspace; do not assume another chat, agent, terminal, or parallel task is visible in the current session. For the development server, also check whether port 9090 is listening and make a lightweight HTTP request to `http://localhost:9090/` before opening a browser page. Reuse a healthy shared `CNSL: Start Development Server`; if a recorded task or process exists but localhost is unresponsive, inspect its output or process state and recover that shared server instead of opening a page that will fail or starting a competing server. For tests, do not start a duplicate or overlapping run when the same relevant suite is already active; reuse its result when available or wait for it to finish, while leaving unrelated active processes untouched.
- **Keep tests loosely coupled and behaviorally strong.** Tests must fail for broken semantic behavior, contracts, accessibility, trust boundaries, or integration wiring, not merely for harmless changes to visitor copy, diagnostic/log prose, markup serialization, presentation structure, or active annual records. Prefer deterministic fixture-owned inputs plus semantic, relational, boundary, negative, and hostile-input assertions. Keep exact values only for stable contracts and dedicated transformation/formatting outputs; follow `.github/instructions/testing.instructions.md` for the complete classification.
- **Run only change-scoped tests locally.** Every local test command must explicitly name the affected unit-test files, browser specs, or stable browser IDs. A focused passing run is final verification for a focused change. Widen only by adding specific tests justified by imports, shared contracts, alternate paths, failures, or observed behavior; never follow a passing focused run with `pnpm test`, `pnpm run test:coverage`, or `pnpm run test:browser:complete` for reassurance. Full suites belong to CI or an explicit user request.
- **Keep changed delivered modules at 100% focused coverage when coverage is needed.** Limit coverage collection to the changed `src/js/**` or service-worker files and execute only their affected tests. Do not collect repository-wide coverage as an automatic completion step.
- **Run `pnpm run lint` after changing executable JavaScript, JavaScript configuration, build scripts, or automation scripts.** Lint failures block the GitHub build and deployment.
- **Run scoped serialized browser verification after significant refactors.** After a refactor changes rendered state, browser interactions, accessibility behavior, or shared browser-facing contracts, run `pnpm run build` followed by the exact affected Playwright specs and stable IDs through `scripts/run-playwright.js`. Add specific sibling or alternate-path IDs when needed; leave the complete workflow and WCAG suite to CI unless explicitly requested.
- **Scale local verification to affected behavior.** Start with the narrowest complete test scope and expand by naming only additional affected tests. Required non-test CI and release gates remain unchanged; complete-suite test execution remains CI-owned.
- **Make retirement part of every refactor.** Before completing a refactor, migration, replacement, or contract change, inventory the superseded implementation and every reference to it across delivered code, alternate execution paths, tests, fixtures, types, adapters, configuration, dependencies, documentation, build validation, and PWA/cache inventories. Remove obsolete paths and their support surface in the same change; do not preserve delivered compatibility merely because a test exercises it, an old comment mentions it, or it might be useful someday. Retain a compatibility path only for a verified current consumer or an explicitly staged migration, and then document its owner, supported scope, removal condition, and focused coverage so it cannot become permanent by accident. Prefer one current contract after migration, update tests to that contract, and verify removed symbols and legacy inputs no longer remain accepted unintentionally. Use the `cnsl-code-retirement` skill for the complete workflow.
- **Retain the attention-banner capability while it is dormant.** The attention banner is an explicitly supported product capability even when `APP_ATTENTION_NOTICE` is `null`, no notice is active, the shared markup is hidden, or prior notice content has expired. Never classify the capability as obsolete from inactivity alone, and do not remove its nullable configuration contract, revision-scoped dismissal storage, controller behavior, semantic markup, theme styles, fixed analytics vocabulary, or focused behavior and accessibility coverage unless the product owner explicitly requests retirement of the capability. Remove expired notice content without restoring it or embedding inactive copy in shared HTML; future notices must continue to use the retained safe runtime-population contract.
- **Continually reduce AI token use through tested automation.** Before asking an agent to discover, compare, classify, or summarize repository facts, prefer a deterministic script, structured query, focused validator, or bounded machine-readable report when the rule can be coded reliably. Run the cheapest deterministic gate first; when it reports no relevant change, stop without loading broad source files. Keep agent context limited to changed records and exact review scopes, and refine recurring workflows so correctness moves from repeated inference into versioned code and tests. Do not trade away correctness, source review, accessibility, security, or required human judgment merely to reduce tokens.
- **Treat performance as a standing engineering requirement.** Every change should preserve or improve visitor-perceived responsiveness, initial delivery cost, rendering work, data-request efficiency, background refresh behavior, and PWA install/update cost. Prefer progressive and asynchronous work when it makes useful content available sooner without weakening accessibility, correctness, offline behavior, or build-version coherence. Avoid duplicate requests, unnecessary full-list rerenders, eager hidden-detail work, and blocking noncritical resources. For changes that materially affect delivered assets, route loading, rendering, annual-data access, weather refresh, or service-worker caching, run `pnpm run measure:performance` with comparable multi-sample `desktop` and `mobile-slow` profiles, compare useful readiness, route phases, request/byte counts, long tasks, and PWA tiers with the recorded baseline, and disclose regressions or unavailable measurements. Separate viewport effects from slower-CPU effects rather than labeling every throttled result as mobile-specific. Keep optimization measurement-led: do not add indexes, caching layers, lazy-loading complexity, or architecture changes without evidence of visitor benefit.
- **Optimize directory routes around useful-content boundaries.** Treat primary annual data readiness, visible summaries, and optional enrichment as distinct measurable phases. When one annual document contains every record, do not introduce per-card network requests merely to call the route lazy-loaded; load the shared primary document once, render lightweight summaries, keep collapsed detail DOM empty, and hydrate only requested or initially expanded details. Keep only summary-critical scripts on the initial route path and load optional detail dependencies once, in explicit classic-script order, with build-version propagation and an accessible settled failure state. Start a deferred route controller as soon as its required DOM and providers are available instead of waiting for `DOMContentLoaded` without a lifecycle need. Consider below-the-fold `content-visibility`, incremental rendering, document splitting, or virtualization only after measurement shows remaining rendering or delivery cost; preserve stable layout, complete keyboard and find-in-page access, deep links, favorites, focus, scroll position, analytics, offline behavior, and full collection filtering/sorting.
- **Drive behavior from semantic data, never presentation.** Business rules, state transitions, filtering, accessibility state, analytics decisions, and interaction behavior must be derived from domain data, typed/status values, or explicit semantic properties. Do not branch on CSS class names, colors, icons, visible labels, tooltip copy, or other rendering choices. Map semantic state to presentation only at the rendering boundary, and add regression coverage when this separation changes behavior.
- **Default to semantic constants and enums; do not introduce hard-coded behavior values.** Before implementing or reviewing behavior, identify every application-owned state, status, action, mode, category, result kind, hazard, event name, storage key, route, duration, or other value used in branching, validation, persistence, rendering decisions, tests, or module-boundary data. Reuse its existing semantic owner first. If none exists, create one: use an immutable enum in `src/js/types/` for a closed related set, a named module-local constant for one implementation value, or shared configuration for a fixed value consumed across broader boundaries. Producers, consumers, validators, rendering maps, caches, and tests must reference that owner rather than repeat or compare raw strings or numbers. Treat a newly repeated behavior literal as a design defect to resolve before completion. Type enum values and carrying objects with JSDoc literal unions or named shapes where practical. Keep display copy at the rendering boundary, and do not mirror annual schema vocabularies, external protocol values, browser platform constants, selectors, or one-off presentation text merely to rename them.
- **Review sibling and alternate execution paths as one behavior constellation.** Before adding or changing behavior, search for every path that produces the same semantic result or maintains the same contract: cached and live, initial and refresh, desktop and mobile, route-specific siblings, manager siblings, source and generated forms, and browser and build/test adapters. When those paths repeat rendering, normalization, validation, timing calculations, accessibility state, error recovery, or collection mechanics, extract the smallest stable shared owner and make each path delegate to it. Keep separate entry points when lifecycle, load timing, runtime, trust boundary, performance role, or domain rules genuinely differ; share their contract rather than forcing them into one module or speculative base class. Add focused contract tests plus at least one integration test for each materially different path.
- **Keep JavaScript contracts documented and code self-explanatory.** Every named class, constructor, method, and function under `src/js/` must retain accurate JSDoc for its purpose, parameters, returned value or promise, observable throws, and privacy boundary where applicable. Keep comments focused on non-obvious intent and constraints; prefer descriptive names, cohesive functions, semantic data, and clear ownership boundaries over narration or vague helpers. Follow `.github/instructions/javascript.instructions.md` for the complete contract.
- **Use CSS for visual spacing, never whitespace characters.** Do not add spaces, nonbreaking spaces, or other whitespace solely to create layout or separation. In particular, link content must not contain leading or trailing visible whitespace, and inline icons must not rely on a literal space before or after their label because that space can inherit underlines or other text decoration. Use an appropriate CSS `gap`, margin, or padding instead, while preserving semantic spaces within visible phrases.
- **Keep SEO metadata separate from concise interface copy.** Put descriptive, seasonal, and location-specific search language in `<title>`, meta descriptions, social metadata, and structured data; do not copy those long phrases into visible page `h1`s, navigation, quick links, buttons, or other compact controls. Visible headings and action labels must remain task-oriented, visually restrained, and compact on supported mobile widths. Follow `.github/instructions/seo.instructions.md` for the complete boundary and verification requirements.
- **Use the approved regular dual-meet window for live meet state.** Standard CNSL regular dual meets run from 7:00 AM through 12:00 PM Eastern so pools can reopen for general public use at noon; live `Upcoming`/`Ongoing` state may use that window. Do not apply this convention to Time Trials, All-City, or other special meets unless their timing is explicitly modeled from an approved source.
- **Use native DOM APIs** consistently with existing route controllers; do not add a DOM library for new behavior without an explicit architecture decision.
- **Keep deployed JavaScript browser-only.** Every file under `src/js/` and published under `out/js/` must be a classic browser script with no CommonJS imports or exports, Node.js runtime globals, environment branches, test harnesses, fixtures, mocks, or test-only adapters. Runtime dependencies come from explicit script order and `globalThis`. Unit tests load browser modules through `tests/helpers/browser-module-loader.js`; genuine Node consumers use narrow adapters under `scripts/adapters/`. Keep Acorn source validation and byte-for-byte copying enabled in the build.
- **No minified libraries** — use full versions for now.
- **Document and rank meaningful features in What's New without rewriting history.** Any meaningful visitor-facing feature addition must include a corresponding update to `src/views/whats-new.html` in the same change. Write each summary and item for families at about an eighth-grade reading level: lead with what visitors will notice, use common words and short sentences, and omit implementation details and technical jargon. Include only enough explanation to understand the change; explain any necessary official or technical term in plain language. Whenever an item is added to `Upcoming` or a release is versioned, reevaluate the full affected list and order it in three contiguous categories: feature additions and improvements first, items labeled `Bug fix` second, and items labeled `Data update` last. Do not add category headings or sections. Within each category, rank items from most to least important to visitors: prioritize safety and correctness, current seasonal information, and broadly used planning workflows; then meaningful reliability, accessibility, privacy, and secondary workflows; then narrow presentation polish. Do not rank mechanically by implementation order or domain labels such as Pool, Meet, and Team. Keep release notes broadly applicable: describe the general capability or visitor benefit, and omit individual team or venue names plus team-specific operational particulars such as exact arrival or warm-up times, parking locations, merchandise locations, concession offerings or payment methods, and similar guidance that applies only to one team or meet. Published wording and item membership are immutable unless the maintainer explicitly requests a historical copy review; such a review may simplify wording but must preserve facts, labels, links, item membership, and release meaning. Unchanged published bullets may be reordered only when the maintainer explicitly requests an importance review. Until functionality is included in a newly published version, document it only in the undated `Upcoming` section.
- **Keep sitemap lastmod values current.** When a change materially updates an indexable public page or a shared public layout, update the relevant `lastmod` entries in `sitemap.xml` to the actual content-change date.
- **Delegate manager-supplied meet guidance.** When a user provides a team-manager welcome email, visiting-team instructions, concessions list, parking or setup guidance, warm-up or check-in details, or another communication intended to update My Meet Day, invoke the `meet-guidance-updater` custom agent as a subagent. That agent owns the active-season data mapping, privacy filtering, provenance, focused regression coverage, verification, and final local My Meet Day outputs for both the home and away teams. Keep broad online source monitoring with `season-data-reviewer`.
- **Delegate significant feature versioning.** When implementing or completing a significant visitor-facing change, invoke the `version-update` custom agent as a subagent to review the current diff and maintain the `Upcoming` note or prepare an explicitly requested new release. Do not bump `APP_VERSION` or create a dated release entry unless release publication is explicitly requested.
- **Summarize refactoring review priorities.** When responding to a refactoring review or audit request, finish with a concise summary of actionable `High`, `Medium`, and `Low` priority items, when any are supported by evidence. If no actionable items are found, state that clearly without introducing placeholder backlog items.
- **Accessibility is required.** All new or modified user-facing experiences must meet WCAG 2.0 Level AA at minimum across the affected page or workflow. Do not complete a site change with known accessibility regressions.
- **Analytics must remain purpose-limited and reportable.** Use Google Analytics only for aggregate public page counts, visit and session reporting, reviewed fixed campaign labels on app-published inbound links, coarse feature-use categories, annual-data-validated favorite pool and team selections, annual-data-validated pool feature filter selections, and annual-data-validated public pool identities for pool-specific interactions. Public pool identities and feature names are directory facts, not visitor-identifying data. Keep `analytics_storage` granted so Google Analytics can use its own first-party analytics identifier for reportable aggregate visits and sessions. Deny advertising storage, Google signals, and advertising/personalization signals. Never send names, contact details, account identifiers, precise or saved location, other selected filter or preference values, free-form/user-provided text, arbitrary URL query/fragment values, referrers, or any app-authored data intended to identify or profile a visitor. Favorite selections, pool feature selections, and reported pool identities must be validated against current published annual data; favorite and pool feature selections may use only a fixed `none` value when cleared. Map approved campaign labels to GA campaign fields only after validating an allowlisted tuple and remove its query tags from the visible URL; do not transmit a full tagged page location. Do not change `analytics_storage` to denied, add app-authored visitor identifiers, or enable additional GA4 enhanced measurement or granular location/device reporting without an explicit privacy review and regression tests.
- **Never navigate an agent-controlled, automated, integrated, or VS Code browser to the production site.** A production page request reaches the origin and can pollute Cloudflare, server, and geographic traffic records even when Google Analytics is disabled or collection requests are intercepted. Exercise browser behavior against the local built artifact with explicit production-origin route fulfillment and blocked external requests. Leave the deployed HTTPS analytics and PWA review to the user in a normal external browser unless the user explicitly directs a production visit and accepts the resulting traffic record.

## Accessibility Requirements

For changes to views, styles, client-side interactions, content, or media:

- Use semantic HTML, logical heading and landmark structure, meaningful link and button text, form labels and instructions, and appropriate alternative text or text alternatives for non-text content.
- Ensure all functionality is operable with a keyboard, has a logical focus order, provides clearly visible focus indication, and does not trap focus unless an accessible modal interaction requires containment and an escape path.
- Preserve sufficient color contrast, do not rely on color or sensory characteristics alone to convey meaning, and avoid content that flashes in ways that may trigger seizures.
- Give controls accessible names, expose state and validation feedback programmatically, and announce important dynamically updated content when needed; prefer native semantics before adding ARIA.
- Keep expand/collapse toggle indicators consistent with Weather Alert: use its filled, decorative `aria-hidden` up-triangle pattern, rotate it 180 degrees without an icon transition when collapsed, and keep it out of the control's accessible name.
- Align inline pills, badges, stars, emoji, and icons with the text beside them from their initial implementation. Prefer baseline-aware inline alignment, verify that marker and text centers read as one horizontal row at desktop and mobile sizes, and avoid leaving markers visibly high or low beside adjacent text.
- When a heading or header begins with an icon, align its supporting content with the start of the header text, not the icon edge. Use a shared CSS grid column, inset, or equivalent responsive layout relationship so each content block remains directly beneath its text label at desktop and mobile sizes; do not approximate this alignment with whitespace characters.
- Provide accessible equivalents for media and time-based content when introduced, including captions or transcripts where applicable.
- Before completing an affected site change, verify the changed workflow with keyboard-only navigation and an appropriate automated accessibility check or browser accessibility inspection; disclose any remaining limitation rather than asserting conformance without verification.

## Code Organization

- JavaScript: `src/js/` with services in `src/js/services/`, models in `src/js/models/`, runtime types in `src/js/types/`, config in `src/js/config/`; schema-shaped design-time JSDoc contracts live in top-level `types/` and are never delivered
- CSS: `src/css/styles.css` (single file, CSS custom properties)
- HTML views: `src/views/*.html` extending `src/views/layouts/base.html`
- HTML components: `src/views/components/` (header, nav, footer, etc.)

## Commands

- `pnpm start` — Clean, build, watch, and serve with live reload
- `pnpm run build` — One-time clean build
- `node --test tests/<area>/<affected-module>.test.js` — Run change-scoped unit tests locally
- `pnpm test` — Run all unit tests in CI or for an explicitly requested full-suite investigation
- `pnpm run test:coverage` — Run full delivered-JavaScript coverage in CI or for an explicitly requested full-suite investigation
- `pnpm run measure:performance` — Clean build plus the repeatable desktop route-phase, request, byte, long-task, and PWA-tier performance report
- `$env:CNSL_PERF_PROFILE='mobile-slow'; $env:CNSL_PERF_RUNS='3'; pnpm run measure:performance` — Repeatable 390px, 4x-CPU diagnostic profile; clear both environment variables afterward
